SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: The paper presents a plausible actor–critic formulation and gives concrete update rules (Equations 6–9 and Algorithm 1) and experimental results on MNIST and CIFAR-10 that qualitatively support the claims. However, there are no theoretical guarantees or analysis of stability (e.g., no discussion of function-approximation instability, target networks, or convergence conditions), and the choice of reward (one-step loss decrement) and state (average minibatch loss) are heuristic and could be noisy. Experimental methodology is reasonable (baselines include tuned SGD, Adam, Adagrad, RMSprop and 5 runs averaged), but lacks statistical tests, detailed hyperparameter settings for the actor/critic, and broader ablations, leaving some claims (robustness/generalization) only partially supported. Overall the approach is technically plausible but has gaps in analysis and experimental rigor.

ORIGINALITY.justification: Applying an actor–critic RL method to learn per-step learning rates is a reasonably novel application of existing RL machinery, and the paper highlights differences from prior adaptive optimizers (e.g., Schaul et al. 2013, Daniel et al. 2016). Nonetheless the contribution is mainly an application/combination of known techniques (deterministic actor–critic, LSTM policy, TD learning) to the learning-rate control problem rather than a fundamentally new algorithmic idea, so the novelty is moderate rather than high.

CLARITY.justification: The paper is organized in the standard structure (intro, related, method, experiments) and presents algorithmic details (Algorithm 1, equations) that make the method reproducible in principle; the experimental setup section specifies model architectures and the concrete state used in experiments. However, the text contains repetition and some missing implementation details (exact actor/critic architectures and training hyperparameters are only partly specified), and several sections (appendix/repeated paragraphs) are cluttered, which reduces readability and leaves some choices insufficiently justified.
